critic_raw:
The Generative Causal Explanations framework claims to provide post-hoc causal explanations by distinguishing causal from non-causal factors in data. The fundamental problem is that 'causal' versus 'non-causal' factors are defined relative to a generative model that is itself learned from observational data. The GCE model cannot identify true causal structure without access to interventional data or a known causal graph; what it actually learns is a statistical factorization that the model parametrizes as 'causal.' On MNIST and fMNIST, the visual factors (digit shape, stroke thickness, clothing type) that are visually intuitive explanations may be correlated with each other in the dataset in ways that make the causal vs. non-causal distinction arbitrary from a purely observational standpoint.

MNIST and fMNIST are extremely low-diversity datasets with clean, centered, grayscale images and near-perfect classification accuracy achievable with simple models. Explanations that look causally meaningful on MNIST may simply reflect that the classifier has learned the same obvious features any human would identify. The benchmark provides no stress test of the causal claim: if the GCE explanation method would produce the same results as a simple saliency map or an attribution method, the causal machinery adds no demonstrable value.

The evaluation is conducted on a single GPU model (Nvidia GeForce GTX 1080), which is not itself a methodological flaw, but it signals that no cross-hardware reproducibility was verified. More importantly, there is no evaluation of explanation stability: if the GCE model is retrained with different seeds, do the identified 'causal' vs. 'non-causal' factor assignments remain consistent? For a generative model with a latent space, different runs can learn permuted or differently structured factorizations that produce qualitatively different explanations while achieving identical reconstruction metrics.

The paper does not specify a quantitative metric for explanation quality that would distinguish causal from non-causal labeling. If the only evaluation is visual inspection of generated counterfactuals, the causal claim is not testable — any generative model that produces visually coherent counterfactuals would appear to satisfy the criterion.
all_issues_raised: The GCE model learns a causal vs. non-causal factorization from observational data without interventional data or a known causal graph, so it identifies a statistical factorization, not genuine causal structure — the causal claim is unfalsifiable given the evaluation setup.

MNIST and fMNIST provide insufficient diversity to stress-test causal explanations; explanations that look causally meaningful on these datasets may be indistinguishable from simple saliency or attribution methods, leaving the causal machinery without demonstrated added value.

Explanation stability across random seeds is not evaluated — generative models can learn permuted latent factorizations across runs that produce qualitatively different causal/non-causal assignments while achieving identical reconstruction metrics.

No quantitative metric distinguishes causal from non-causal labeling quality; if evaluation relies on visual inspection of generated counterfactuals, any generative model producing coherent counterfactuals satisfies the criterion.

MNIST and fMNIST provide insufficient diversity to stress-test causal explanations; explanations that look causally meaningful on these datasets may be indistinguishable from simple saliency or attribution methods, leaving the causal machinery without demonstrated added value.

No quantitative metric distinguishes causal from non-causal labeling quality; if evaluation relies on visual inspection of generated counterfactuals, any generative model producing coherent counterfactuals satisfies the criterion.
